attributes.stem:
A 72-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic for routine follow-up of his longstanding hypertension and dyslipidaemia. He has no other significant medical history. His regular medications include perindopril, simvastatin, and aspirin. He drinks 15 g of alcohol per day and stopped smoking 20 years ago.

On examination, he appears well. His pulse rate is 74/min, blood pressure 145/86 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.5°C. His BMI is 28 kg/m². A systolic bruit is auscultated over the right common carotid artery; otherwise, the cardiac examination is unremarkable. Duplex ultrasonography confirms the presence of a 70% stenosis of the right carotid artery and 20% stenosis of the left carotid artery.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Optimise medical therapy and lifestyle modifications
attributes.explanation:
This patient has asymptomatic carotid artery stenosis, as there is no history of stroke, transient ischaemic attack, or neurological symptoms. Current Australian and international guidelines recommend optimise medical therapy and lifestyle modifications as the first-line management for asymptomatic carotid stenosis, even when the stenosis is severe (>70%). Whilst Dr Google may have suggested that something more active might have been needed, these patients need firm reassurance that in the absence of symptoms they will not benefit from more active intervention, where the risks of the procedure may outweigh the gains.

This approach focuses on controlling modifiable risk factors and includes:

  • Antiplatelet therapy

  • High dose statins

  • Intensive blood pressure control, and

  • Lifestyle changes such as weight management, smoking cessation, and regular exercise.

Right sided carotid endarterectomy is not recommended for asymptomatic patients according to current Australian Stroke Foundation guidelines. While older studies suggested some benefit of surgery in asymptomatic patients with severe stenosis, more recent evidence and guidelines emphasize that the small absolute risk reduction for stroke with surgery does not outweigh the perioperative risks in asymptomatic individuals. Carotid endarterectomy is primarily indicated for symptomatic patients with recent (within 3 months) stroke or TIA and ipsilateral carotid stenosis of 50-99%, with the greatest benefit seen in those with 70-99% stenosis.

Right carotid stent is similarly not indicated for asymptomatic carotid stenosis. Carotid artery stenting may be considered as an alternative to endarterectomy in symptomatic patients who are at high risk for surgery, but it carries its own set of procedural risks and is not recommended for asymptomatic patients.

Bilateral carotid stenting would expose the patient to unnecessary procedural risks without clear benefit. There is no indication for intervention on the left carotid artery with only 20% stenosis, and as discussed, intervention is not recommended for the asymptomatic right carotid stenosis either.

Bilateral carotid endarterectomy would be an excessive intervention with no evidence-based justification. This would expose the patient to significant perioperative risks without proven benefit in the asymptomatic setting.

The management of this patient should focus on optimizing his current medical therapy (ensuring adequate doses of antihypertensives and statins), addressing his elevated blood pressure (145/86 mmHg), encouraging weight loss to address his overweight status (BMI 28 kg/m²), and continuing regular surveillance of the carotid stenosis. This approach aligns with current best practice guidelines for asymptomatic carotid stenosis.
learningPoints: Asymptomatic carotid artery stenosis is generally managed with best medical therapy rather than surgical intervention.
Best medical therapy for carotid stenosis includes antiplatelet agents, statins, blood pressure control, and lifestyle modifications.
Carotid endarterectomy is primarily indicated for symptomatic patients with significant stenosis, not for asymptomatic individuals.
references: Stroke Foundation. Australian and New Zealand living stroke guidelines: carotid intervention. Melbourne: Stroke Foundation; 2022. Available at: https://app.magicapp.org/#/guideline/8L0RME/section/LwgvpL

Galyfos G, Sachsamanis G, Anastasiadou C, Sachmpazidis I, Kikiras K, Kastrisios G et al. Carotid Endarterectomy versus Carotid Stenting or Best Medical Treatment in Asymptomatic Patients with Significant Carotid Stenosis: A meta-analysis. Cardiovascular Revascularization Medicine. 2019;20(5):413-423.

Ederle J, Bonati L, Dobson J, Featherstone R, Gaines P, Beard J et al. Endovascular treatment with angioplasty or stenting versus endarterectomy in patients with carotid artery stenosis in the Carotid And Vertebral Artery Transluminal Angioplasty Study (CAVATAS): long-term follow-up of a randomised trial. The Lancet Neurology. 2009;8(10):898-907.
Faber A. Modern Treatment of Asymptomatic Carotid Stenosis - The Importance of Both Medical Therapy and Carotid Endarterectomy.JAMA Surgery 2022; 157:653-54.
